stories.title: $25 million healthcare center to expand access to tribal communities
stories.summary: The Fond du Lac Band broke ground on a $25 million Center for American Indian Resources in Duluth. The 33,000 sq. ft. facility will expand mental, social, and dental services for tribal members, addressing a critical need—especially for dental care, where the current ratio is one dentist per 2,000 patients.
stories.extended_summary: In Duluth, a new $25 million healthcare center is rising for the Fond du Lac Band. The Center for American Indian Resources will offer mental, social, and dental services in an urban setting, where access has been limited. Last year, the Cloquet district had a staggering 1-to-2,000 patient-to-dentist ratio. This 33,000-square-foot facility aims to change that, bringing comprehensive care closer to home for Native people across the region.
stories.display_reason: hero always uses image mode (summary always visible)

stories.fake_headline: New Program Trains Deaf Baristas in Sign Language Coffee Shops Across State

stories.title: 'Suffering' South Dakota Dog Adopted & Now Thriving At New Home
stories.summary: Monk, a severely neglected dog with matted fur, overgrown nails, and visible ribs, arrived at the Sioux Falls Area Humane Society. Despite his suffering, he showed trust. After a sedated shave and intensive care, Monk was adopted by a loving family and is now thriving in his new home.
stories.extended_summary: Monk came to the Sioux Falls Area Humane Society as a broken dog—so heavily matted his fur was filled with fecal material, his nails painfully overgrown, and his waist only three inches across. Yet he greeted everyone with gentle hugs. The team sedated him to remove the mats and found starvation underneath. They nursed him back to health, and today Monk has a forever family who gives him all the love he deserves.
stories.headline_split: 'Suffering' South Dakota Dog Adopted & Now Thriving
stories.fake_headline: Lost Kitten Found After 3 Days Trapped Inside Library Book Return Slot

stories.title: 'They left me no other option': Reedley farmer gives 125,000+ pounds of nectarines away amid lawsuit
stories.summary: After being sued by a large ag corporation for allegedly breaking a contract, Reedley farmer Cesar Mora decided to give away over 125,000 pounds of nectarines for free. Thousands of people have already shown up to collect the fruit, turning a legal dispute into a community windfall.
stories.extended_summary: Cesar Mora, a third-generation farmer, was sued by Giumarra Brothers Fruit Company for sending his nectarines to another buyer. Instead of letting the crop go to waste, he posted videos on Instagram and started giving them away. The response was overwhelming—over 866,000 views and lines of people with buckets. Mora says the corporation left him no choice, but the community is reaping the sweetness of his defiance.
stories.headline_split: 'They left me no other option': Reedley farmer gives 125,000+ pounds of nectarines away
stories.fake_headline: Teen Inventor Creates Edible Water Bottles From Seaweed, Wins $50,000 Prize

stories.title: A cultural beacon: Morning Star American Indian Village to open at Cheyenne Frontier Days
stories.summary: Cheyenne Frontier Days is opening the Morning Star American Indian Village, a permanent 3.8-acre cultural landmark. Featuring a sunken dance arbor, artisan booths, storytelling spaces, and a veterans memorial, the village honors American Indian performers who have been part of the event since 1897.
stories.extended_summary: For the first time in its 130-year history, Cheyenne Frontier Days will have a permanent American Indian Village. Dancers will enter from the east, as tradition dictates—a small but meaningful change. The 3.8-acre site includes a dance arbor, teepee, native plants, and educational signs. It replaces the old village and expands space for performers, artisans, and storytellers, creating a cultural beacon for the community.
stories.headline_split: A cultural beacon: Morning Star American Indian Village to open at

stories.title: A different — delicious — kind of lab in Rochester
stories.summary: Kelly Rae Kirkpatrick hosts farm-to-table dinners in her urban garden in Rochester, using ingredients she grows herself. Her Garden Lab is more than a business—it's a movement that also runs a seed library that gave away 20,000 packets this year, all while supporting local organizations.
stories.extended_summary: Under a perfect summer sky, guests gather in Kelly Rae Kirkpatrick's backyard for a dinner unlike any other. Every ingredient comes from her urban farmstead—30 vegetables, herbs, fruit trees, and 15 varieties of flowers. She co-founded the Seed Library at Rochester Public Library, which distributed 20,000 packets this year. Her CSA bouquets and donations to local organizations make her garden a hub of generosity and education.

stories.title: A major American milestone was the perfect time for Studyville founder Amanda Martin to write her first children’s book
stories.summary: Studyville founder Amanda Martin wrote 'Henry the Star-Spangled Spaniel' to teach civics to elementary students. The book, published in time for America's 250th birthday, follows a time-traveling dog who witnesses the signing of the Declaration of Independence. It's already kid-tested by 4,000 students.
stories.extended_summary: Amanda Martin never planned to write a children's book, but when her tutoring company needed early civics resources, she created a character that stuck. Henry the Star-Spangled Spaniel, based on her own Cavalier King Charles Spaniel, travels back in time in a red, white, and blue doghouse. With Simon & Schuster publishing it, the book brings history to life for young readers—and it's already a hit with thousands of kids.

stories.title: AARP Grant to Fund Walking Trail and Grounds at HCCOA
stories.summary: Hardy County Committee on Aging received a $15,000 AARP grant to build a walking trail with benches on an unused acre of land. The trail will provide a safe, short path for seniors who find area parks too large and tiring to navigate.
stories.extended_summary: For many seniors in Hardy County, a 'walk in the park' is literally out of reach—the existing trails are too long and exhausting. So the Committee on Aging is transforming an overgrown acre into a short walking loop with benches. A $15,000 AARP grant covers construction, and the community is stepping up to donate additional benches. The trail will offer gentle exercise and a place to gather, all under the shade of a weathered gazebo.

stories.title: After giving away food on the sly for years, community rallies to help ‘Hot Dog Lou’ stay in his home
stories.summary: Lou Cassaniti, 83, known as 'Hot Dog Lou,' gave away food to anyone in need for years. Now he's fallen behind on property taxes and bills, and the community has rallied, raising over $10,000 through a GoFundMe to help him stay in his home.
stories.extended_summary: For decades, Lou Cassaniti quietly fed the hungry from his street food stand on the Ithaca Commons. But as his business declined, the 83-year-old found himself struggling to pay his own bills. When word got out, the community he served for so long turned around to help him. A GoFundMe raised more than $10,000 from over 170 donors, including two $1,000 gifts. Lou says he's shocked that strangers would give so much, but to them, he's family.
stories.headline_split: After giving away food on the sly for years, community rallies to help ‘Hot Dog Lou’

stories.title: An idea worth wearing: UWM alum creates jewelry company based on an inclusive design
stories.summary: UWM alum Kayla Lokker turned a frustration with necklace clasps into Easy Wear Jewelry, featuring her patented Easy Clasp. The design was inspired by retractable ID holders and makes putting on necklaces effortless—a simple solution to an everyday problem.
stories.extended_summary: Kayla Lokker was always late because she couldn't fasten her necklace clasps fast enough. An entrepreneurship lesson urged her to solve everyday frustrations, and she created Easy Clasp—a mechanism adapted from retractable badge holders. Now her startup, Easy Wear Jewelry, offers necklaces that anyone can put on in seconds. Lokker's journey from a gymnast training 40 hours a week to a patent-holding founder shows how persistence pays off.

stories.title: Army’s only airborne rabbi finds his congregation wherever he lands
stories.summary: Rabbi Scott Klein is the Army's only airborne Jewish chaplain, serving at Fort Bragg. He jumps out of planes and wraps tefillin with soldiers before dawn, finding his congregation wherever he lands—providing spiritual support to Jewish troops in one of the most unexpected places.
stories.extended_summary: At 36, Rabbi Scott Klein serves the world's largest military base as the Army's only parachute-qualified Jewish chaplain. That means he's ready to jump from a plane—literally—to be with his soldiers. One morning, after PT, he helped Specialist Jacob Abrams wrap tefillin in a battalion meeting room. For soldiers like Abrams, who discovered Jewish life on base by chance, Klein's presence is a lifeline. He brings ancient rituals to modern warfare, drenched in sweat and airborne dust.

stories.title: Arnoldsville Fire Department expands its station for firefighters, community
stories.summary: The all-volunteer Arnoldsville Fire Department is adding a new building with air conditioning, an ADA bathroom, and a backup generator. The expansion will serve as a community shelter during severe weather and provide more space for training and operations.
stories.extended_summary: After taking out its first loan since 2010, the Arnoldsville Fire Department is building a 30-by-40 foot side addition with air conditioning, a kitchen, and an ADA-compliant bathroom. The station lacked AC before. With a planned backup generator, it will double as a refuge for the community during natural disasters or long power outages. Chief Heath Baker says costs outpaced fundraising, but the crew pushed forward. Locally owned Fortis Structure is handling construction without disrupting emergency response.

stories.title: Being Neighborly | A store manager who refused to make a sale
stories.summary: When Safeway manager TJ Faulkner noticed a customer trying to buy thousands of dollars in gift cards, he stepped in. He patiently explained the scam, and with help from another shopper, convinced the man he was being tricked out of $100,000.
stories.extended_summary: A scammer told a man in his 70s that buying $100,000 in gift cards would unlock a $152 million payout. At the Safeway on Point Fosdick Drive, manager TJ Faulkner refused to ring up the purchase. Instead, he peppered the customer with questions, joined by shopper Taryn Thibaut. The man had already bought thousands at other stores. Their calm persistence broke through, and he left without handing over more money—a financial rescue that could have ruined him.
stories.headline_split: Being Neighborly | A store manager who refused
fake_endings: to accept a tip from a grateful customer
to let a shoplifter go free

stories.title: Beloved Man honored in Hayesville
stories.summary: Davy Arch, Beloved Man of the Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians, was honored at the Moss Memorial Library in Hayesville, NC. Darry Wood presented him with a blanket and unveiled a display featuring a mannequin in 1800s Cherokee clothing, funded by Arch himself after grants fell through.
stories.extended_summary: When a grant for a historical display fell through, Beloved Man Davy Arch told the creator, 'Go ahead and build it. I will fund it.' That display—a full-sized mannequin in 1800s Cherokee clothing with a blowgun—was unveiled during an open house at Moss Memorial Library. Arch, a respected cultural leader, was then presented with a blanket by Darry Wood. The event celebrated Cherokee heritage and the generosity of a man who quietly ensures traditions live on.
stories.headline_split: Beloved Man
stories.fake_headline: Teenager Builds 300 Birdhouses to Boost Local Ecosystem After Learning About Bee Decline

stories.title: Birmingham’s ‘Melanated Reader’ Crystal Forte is Leading a Movement One Black Book at a Time
stories.summary: Crystal Forte, the Birmingham-based @melanatedreader, launched the Reading Black Books Summer Reading Club with a goal of getting one million people to read a book by a Black author. She describes it as a mission to shift the culture of literacy.
stories.extended_summary: Crystal Forte wants one million people to read one Black-authored book this summer. The high school student support specialist and literary influencer started the Reading Black Books Summer Reading Club to tackle what she calls a literacy crisis. Her campaign is about more than numbers—it's about access, representation, and honoring elders like her grandmother, a Gee's Bend quilter. Forte is building community one recommendation at a time, and her online movement is growing fast.
stories.headline_split: Birmingham’s ‘Melanated Reader’ Crystal Forte is Leading a Movement

stories.title: Black Mom of 3 Launches Free New Scholarship Program to Help 25,000 Black Families Send Their Kids to College
stories.summary: After losing her mother at 16 and missing out on Spelman, India Brown created the Legacy Leaders Scholarship Program. It's free, has no essays or income limits, and aims to help 25,000 Black families earn guaranteed scholarship rewards for college.
stories.extended_summary: India Brown was accepted to Spelman College at 16, but after her mother died without life insurance, she couldn't afford to go. Decades later, she turned that heartbreak into action. The Legacy Leaders Scholarship Program gives families free access to one of the largest private college tuition reward networks, covering 440+ schools including HBCUs. No essays, no income checks—just a path to guaranteed scholarship dollars. Her goal: 25,000 Black families get college funding they can start earning years early.
stories.headline_split: Black Mom of 3 Launches Free New Scholarship Program

stories.title: Black Postal Worker Saved an 89-Year-Old Woman's Life After Noticing She Hadn't Collected Her Mail in 3 Days
stories.summary: Chicago mail carrier Shonda Lemon noticed an 89-year-old woman hadn't collected her mail for three days. Concerned, she requested a welfare check and found Helen on her floor, dehydrated and unable to get help. Her quick thinking saved a life.
stories.extended_summary: Shonda Lemon, a USPS carrier in Chicago's Dunning neighborhood, had been bundling Helen's mail with rubber bands to make it easier for her. When those bundles sat untouched for three days, she knocked, then called emergency services. Responders found Helen on the floor, days without food or water. Her niece said she feared the worst. Lemon's simple act of noticing—and caring—made the difference between a tragedy and a rescue that brought Helen to safety.
stories.headline_split: Black Postal Worker Saved an 89-Year-Old Woman's Life After Noticing

stories.title: California Woman’s Doorbell Cam Shows Teen Doing the ‘Right Thing’ and Walking Off—His Mom Unexpectedly Earned Him a $3,000 Reward
stories.summary: When 17-year-old Adrian Rodriguez found a purse in a shopping cart, he drove to the address on the ID to return it. His good deed was caught on doorbell cam, and the owner's friend set up a GoFundMe that raised over $3,000 for the teen.
stories.extended_summary: Adrian Rodriguez didn't think twice about returning a lost purse he found in a shopping cart. He drove to the address on the driver's license, handed it to an elderly woman—captured on Ring camera. But the purse belonged to her niece, who had moved out. When the real owner learned what happened, she and her friend tracked Adrian down via social media. They started a GoFundMe hoping for $1,000; it blew past $3,000. His response? 'My mom raised me to do the right thing even when no one's watching.'

stories.title: Citizen of the Week: Natasha Agarwal
stories.summary: During lockdown, Natasha Agarwal learned that kids just 10 miles away lacked books. She started a book drive that collected 3,000 books, then founded Believe in Reading. Five years later, her nonprofit has distributed thousands of books and started literacy programs.
stories.extended_summary: Natasha Agarwal's bookshelves were full, but during the pandemic she discovered a 'book desert' just 10 miles away. Her first book drive for a local early childhood center pulled in 3,000 books. That one-time project turned into Believe in Reading, a youth-run nonprofit. Now a rising sophomore at Penn, Natasha has run multiple drives and started literacy programs serving low-income areas. In Philadelphia, where 71% of fourth graders read below grade level, her work is closing gaps one book at a time.

stories.title: Colorado mountain towns must tighten taps, but water shortages could have been much worse
stories.summary: Facing historic water shortages, Colorado's River District is urgently shifting water from reservoirs to towns and ranches most at risk. By prioritizing people over wealthy ranchers, they're ensuring drinking water for communities that could otherwise go dry.
stories.extended_summary: Historic drought is strangling Colorado's mountain towns. The Colorado River District, with nearly $1 million in support, is doling out stored water not to the highest bidder but based on need. They turned down wealthy 'gentlemen ranchers' to reserve water for drinking and food production. Emergency orders allow unprecedented flexibility. It's a rescue mission for communities that can't afford to lose their taps—and a model for managing scarcity with fairness.

stories.title: Confident Is This Head That Wears the Crown
stories.summary: Alhambra High grad Keiko Rakin is the first student from her district to join the Tournament of Roses Court. After advocating to expand eligibility boundaries, she now heads to Wellesley College, inspired by Hillary Clinton and Madeleine Albright.
stories.extended_summary: Keiko Rakin lived just outside the Rose Court eligibility zone—so she wrote a letter asking them to expand. They did. She became Alhambra Unified's first Rose Princess. But that's just one chapter. As student school board member and chair of the superintendent's advisory council, she's been breaking barriers for years. Now off to Wellesley, she's following in the footsteps of two secretaries of state. Her crown is confidence, and she's just getting started.

stories.title: Copters carry logs into river for salmon
stories.summary: Helicopters are airlifting massive logs into the Toutle River to rebuild salmon habitat. The project uses woody debris to create hiding spots for juvenile fish and restore the river's natural flow. It's a high-tech solution for a watershed that supports six endangered species.
stories.extended_summary: The whomp-whomp of helicopter blades echoes across the South Fork Toutle River valley as 25-foot logs are carried upstream. Crews from Columbia Helicopters are placing woody debris in a habitat-restoration project that benefits chinook, steelhead, and other endangered salmon. The logs slow the water and provide crucial cover for young fish. The Lower Columbia Fish Enhancement Group bought a $2 million timber harvest to get the logs to this remote location. The work is part of a larger effort to restore side channels and native plants in one of the few watersheds that hosts all listed salmon species.

stories.title: Cue the Instagram Photos: There Are Finally Peachtrees on on Peachtree
stories.summary: For the first time, actual peach trees are growing on Peachtree Street in downtown Atlanta. Pearson Farm donated 16 summer gold peach trees to Underground Atlanta and Woodruff Park. The whimsical planting is timed to welcome visitors for the 2026 FIFA World Cup.
stories.extended_summary: After decades of joke-worthy irony, peach trees finally line Peachtree Street in Atlanta. A local creative's poem about 'growing peachtrees on Peachtree' sparked a real-life initiative. Nonprofit Atlanta Way 2.0 partnered with fifth-generation Pearson Farm to transplant 16 dormant trees in February. The trees now stand at Underground Atlanta and Woodruff Park, giving downtown its first peach grove. The project aims to fix a long-standing oddity and impress World Cup fans.

stories.title: Dad Gave Up His Old Mustang to Raise 6 Kids. Decades Later, His Son Got it Back
stories.summary: Shane Allen spent years searching for his father's dream car, a 1969 Mustang Mach 1 that was traded away to make room for six kids. When he found one, he traded his own restored Mustang for it. On Father's Day, he surprised his dad, Daniel, with the exact car he'd described for decades.
stories.extended_summary: Shane Allen never forgot his father Daniel's story about the white 1969 Mustang Mach 1 he traded for a family car. After returning from Vietnam, Daniel bought that car, proposed in it, and drove it until the kids came. Shane, the youngest son, grew up bonding with his dad over classic cars at Father's Day car shows. This year, he spotted a white Mach 1—just like the one his father missed—and traded his own restored Mustang to get it. The surprise gift on Father's Day brought the car back full circle.

stories.title: Detroit Breaks Ground on $20M Brennan Community Center for Cody Rouge Neighborhood
stories.summary: Detroit broke ground on a $20 million community center in Rouge Park, bringing year-round recreation to the Cody Rouge neighborhood. The 25,000-square-foot Brennan Community Center is fully funded by Pistons owner Tom Gores. It will replace the district's lack of indoor space for youth, families, and seniors.
stories.extended_summary: For years, District 7 was the only Detroit district without an indoor community center—until now. The Brennan Community Center broke ground on June 26, 2026 and is expected to open in fall 2027. Pistons owner Tom Gores covered the entire $20 million cost. The center will offer pools, a gym, and gathering spaces that stay open all year. Former Pistons coach Dwane Casey said the idea came from asking players how they wanted to help their community.

stories.title: Drag queen baristas to ‘serve’ at local coffee shop
stories.summary: Charlottesville's Tribute Coffee is hosting drag queen baristas for Pride Month. Jackie Of All Trades and Darling Nikki will serve coffee and raise money for Fight Like a GRRRL, which offers free self-defense workshops. Twenty percent of sales will be donated to help expand the program.
stories.extended_summary: Sunday at Tribute Coffee, the baristas are serving looks alongside lattes. Drag queens Jackie Of All Trades and Darling Nikki take over the espresso machine for a fundraiser benefiting Fight Like a GRRRL. The nonprofit runs free self-defense workshops across Central Virginia, and demand has skyrocketed recently. Owner Jordan Kessler envisioned the event as a fun way to give back, and the shop already pays a living wage. Tips for the drag queens are encouraged—and 20% of the day's gross sales will go to training more instructors.

stories.title: Farewell, fines — Jefferson-Madison Regional Library to drop overdue fees for most items starting July 1
stories.summary: Jefferson-Madison Regional Library is eliminating overdue fines for most items starting July 1. The move also clears past fines, so frozen library cards will work again. Only bestsellers, toys, and kits will still carry late fees.
stories.extended_summary: JMRL is joining the majority of U.S. libraries by ditching overdue fines on most items. Starting July 1, patrons won't pay 25 cents per day—and existing fines vanish, reactivating blocked cards. High-demand items like bestsellers and kits still have fees. Director David Plunkett said the library has been considering this for years as research shows fines create barriers without improving return rates. Patrons are still responsible for lost or damaged items, but returning a billed item clears the account.

stories.title: Finding respite during the holiday rush
stories.summary: The Franciscan Place Chapel at Destiny USA mall offers shoppers a quiet escape for prayer and reflection. Staffed by Franciscan friars, it provides Mass and the Sacrament of Reconciliation. Nearly 2,000 people have received services there this year, finding peace in the middle of the holiday rush.
stories.extended_summary: In the midst of Destiny USA's shopping frenzy, a tiny chapel offers silence. The Franciscan Place, near DSW Shoes, is staffed by Franciscan friars who model St. Francis's ministry in the marketplace. Since January, 1,842 people have received the Sacrament of Reconciliation and 2,045 have attended Mass there. The chapel is a favorite for regulars and weary shoppers alike. Director Brother Jim Moore says it provides 'a quiet moment to pray, reflect and take a break from our busy lives,' especially during the holidays.

stories.title: Freight Company, Farm in Shamong Collaborate  to Give Away 41,000 Lbs.  of Cucumbers Rejected  After Load Shift in Transit
stories.summary: After a load shift forced a truck to reject 41,000 pounds of cucumbers, a Shamong farm partnered with the driver to give them away for free. Volunteers bagged and distributed the cucumbers to the community over two days. What could have been waste turned into a massive free produce event.
stories.extended_summary: Driver Mikey PauPaw braked to avoid a deer, causing his cucumber load to shift. The pickle company rejected the shipment over packaging damage, but the cucumbers themselves were fine. Instead of dumping them, PauPaw and Piney Pig & Poultry Farm organized a giveaway. Volunteers shoveled cucumbers into bags, urging neighbors to take as many as they could carry. In two days, all 41,000 pounds found homes, turning a near-disaster into a community feast.

stories.title: From parking lot to party spread: How a Circleville restaurant came full circle for local family’s adoption journey
stories.summary: When the Pitzer family adopted baby Allan Ray, the process began in the parking lot of a Circleville Buffalo Wild Wings. Two years later, they celebrated his adoption finalization at the same restaurant. The staff threw them a party, bringing their journey full circle.
stories.extended_summary: Allan Ray came home from the NICU in December 2023 when his parents met a caseworker in a Buffalo Wild Wings parking lot. The family visited so often that Allan's excited arm flapping earned him the nickname 'little chicken wing.' On March 9, 2026, his adoption was finalized. Nathan Pitzer emailed the restaurant to share the news, and the staff surprised the family with a full celebration. The same restaurant that hosted the beginning of their journey now hosted the happy ending.

stories.title: From prison tablets to Cleveland kitchens: How EDWINS has expanded its culinary training to nearly every state
stories.summary: Drew Hanchett found a culinary curriculum on his tablet in a county jail in Arizona. After completing it, he was flown to Cleveland for in-person training at EDWINS Leadership & Restaurant Institute. The program, which started with tablet courses in prisons, has expanded to nearly every state and offers a path out of incarceration.
stories.extended_summary: Behind bars in Tucson, Drew Hanchett devoured videos on knife skills and butchery through Edovo, an education app on prison tablets. He didn't know the courses were from EDWINS, a Cleveland program that flies graduates out for hands-on training. EDWINS founder Brandon Chrostowski, once arrested as a teen, built the institute to prove culinary training can break the cycle of incarceration. The Cleveland Browns sponsor flights for graduates. Hanchett now works in a professional kitchen—a journey that started with a tablet in a cell.
stories.headline_split: From prison tablets to Cleveland kitchens: How EDWINS has
fake_endings: created a culinary school in every state
trained inmates to become professional chefs

stories.title: Goodwill celebrates inaugural class of Excel Center graduates in Rockford
stories.summary: After four decades and four tries, 60-year-old Willma Cooper finally earned her high school diploma through Goodwill's Excel Center in Rockford. She was one of 46 graduates in the inaugural class, proving it's never too late to change your life's direction.
stories.extended_summary: "I finally reached the end of the tunnel and I made it," said Willma Cooper, 60, after earning her high school diploma on her fourth attempt. She was celebrated Tuesday as part of the first graduating class of the Excel Center, a tuition-free adult high school run by Goodwill. The school opened less than a year ago and offers flexible classes and free child care for adult learners facing barriers. Cooper's journey shows that obstacles like raising kids and financial struggles can be overcome.
stories.headline_split: Goodwill celebrates inaugural class of Excel Center graduates

stories.title: Hamtramck Program Reaches 400 Trees Planted in Push for 1,000 by 2028
stories.summary: The Grow Hamtramck program has planted 400 trees across Michigan's most densely populated city, with a goal of 1,000 by 2028. The initiative relies on resident requests and focuses on species diversity to create shade, reduce heat, and improve pedestrian safety.
stories.extended_summary: Hamtramck's urban canopy is growing fast — 400 trees planted so far, with 600 more on the way by 2028. The grant-funded Grow Hamtramck program targets 1,000 total trees in Michigan's most densely populated city. An initial inventory found over 1,200 available planting spots on public property. Workers choose small varieties for narrow spaces and prioritize residential streets to cut heat and slow traffic. Species diversity is key to keeping the canopy healthy.
stories.headline_split: Hamtramck Program Reaches 400 Trees Planted
fake_endings: and a state grant for more
stories.fake_headline: Kindergarten Class Starts Garden, Donates Vegetables to Food Bank

stories.title: Have You Heard of the Pawtucket Slaterettes?
stories.summary: The Pawtucket Slaterettes, founded in 1973 as the Darlington Pioneers, are the oldest all-female recreational baseball league in the country. What started with four senior and six junior teams now includes divisions from T-ball to adults.
stories.extended_summary: Jo-Ann Wunschel grew up in a baseball family and had no interest in softball — so her parents started a girls' baseball league in 1973. The Darlington Pioneers soon became the Pawtucket Slaterettes, the oldest all-female recreational baseball league in the U.S. In the early days, they played Sunday mornings around the boys' Little League schedule. Today, the league serves players from T-ball through adults at Fairlawn Veterans Memorial Park.
stories.headline_split: Have You Heard of
stories.fake_headline: Local Barber Gives Free Haircuts to Homeless Every Sunday

stories.title: How five Cleveland health systems collaborated to boost lead screening
stories.summary: Five Cleveland health systems now screen kids for lead poisoning with a finger prick during checkups, replacing the need for a separate lab visit. The collaborative effort makes testing easier for families, catching lead exposure that can cause irreversible brain damage.
stories.extended_summary: Five-year-old Jada gets a finger prick and a My Little Pony sticker — but what just happened could protect her brain for life. Twenty-one practices across Northeast Ohio have started screening kids for lead with finger pricks during wellness visits, part of a collaboration among major health systems. Previously, kids had to go to a lab for a blood draw, a barrier that many families couldn't overcome. Lead poisoning can cause irreversible cognitive delays, so making the test part of a routine checkup is a game-changer.
stories.headline_split: How five Cleveland health systems collaborated
stories.fake_headline: How a City's Public Libraries Started Offering Free Health Screenings

stories.title: How the Bronx School of Hip-Hop uses the borough’s signature genre to teach lessons far beyond it
stories.summary: The Bronx School of Hip-Hop will welcome its first ninth-grade class as the only noncharter public school in the nation using hip-hop across every subject. Principal Jason Reyes says it's about using hip-hop as a rigorous academic framework to engage students.
stories.extended_summary: When people hear 'hip-hop school,' they think music production — but the Bronx School of Hip-Hop is different. It's a public high school that weaves hip-hop into literature, math, science, civics, and art. Founding principal Jason Reyes, a Bronx native, saw attendance and achievement rise at his middle school when he rooted education in hip-hop culture. The school, opening this fall, aims to change how students see themselves and their learning potential.
stories.headline_split: How the Bronx School of Hip-Hop uses the borough’s signature genre to teach lessons
stories.fake_headline: High School Seniors Start Podcast to Discuss Mental Health and Inspire Peers

stories.title: How two Minnesota teens saved a life with skills they never imagined using
stories.summary: Two 19-year-olds, Matias Vega and Eoin Porrata, used CPR training from a lifeguard course to save their friend's father after he collapsed on a pontoon boat. What started as a goofing-off trip turned into a life-or-death moment where their skills mattered.
stories.extended_summary: It was a beautiful July day on the Mississippi, and a group of friends had just returned from exploring an island carrying a big log. Then Luis Calderon suddenly slumped over in the pontoon. At first, Vega and Porrata thought it was a joke — but when his wife and daughter started crying, they realized it was real. The two 19-year-olds, who had just gotten CPR training for a lifeguard job, jumped into action. They performed chest compressions until paramedics arrived, and Calderon survived.
stories.display_reason: medium image but highly visual story
stories.headline_split: How two Minnesota teens saved a life
fake_endings: by acting quickly at a lake
stories.fake_headline: Three-Year-Old Calls 911 to Save Mom, Becomes Youngest Hero in Town

stories.title: Humpback whale freed from entanglement in Endicott Arm
stories.summary: A juvenile humpback whale entangled in commercial crab pot lines near Endicott Arm was freed by a NOAA response team. The lines ran through the whale's mouth and wrapped around its tail, effectively hog-tying it until rescuers arrived.
stories.extended_summary: Trapped by two 800-pound crab pots, the young humpback whale could barely move. Lines ran through its mouth and baleen, then tangled around its tail — 'hog-tied,' as one marine mammal specialist described it. The whale could only use its pectoral fins to reach the surface to breathe. A multi-agency team trained by NOAA's Large Whale Entanglement Program mobilized from Juneau and successfully cut the lines, freeing the whale in a dramatic rescue that depended on quick action from mariners who spotted it first.
stories.headline_split: Humpback whale freed from entanglement
stories.fake_headline: Stranded Dolphin Rescued by Surfers and Returned to Ocean

stories.title: In pictures: Youngsters reel in a good time at 19th annual KCB Kids Fishing Derby
stories.summary: The 19th annual Key Colony Beach Kids Fishing Derby brought 45 junior anglers to the Marathon Lady, raising over $30,000 for local children. The event has grown from a $2,000 idea into a summer mainstay with trophies and toys for participants.
stories.extended_summary: What started with $2,000 and a police chief's dream is now a five-figure celebration of fishing and community. On June 17, 45 kids dropped lines off the Marathon Lady, competing for the biggest catch at the Ron Sutton Memorial Kids Fishing Derby in Key Colony Beach. Afterward, they piled into the Inn at Key Colony for lunch and towering trophies. Chief Kris DiGiovanni has shepherded the event for 19 years, and this year it netted more than $30,000 in donations for prizes and support for children in need.
stories.headline_split: In pictures: Youngsters reel in a good time
stories.fake_headline: Elementary Students Create Art Murals to Beautify Neighborhood Streets

stories.title: Insurance Doesn’t Cover Wheelchair Ramps. In Idaho, a Church Group Finds a Solution.
stories.summary: When Melinda Nelson's insurance wouldn't cover the $20,000 cost of a wheelchair ramp, a local church group called Love INC stepped in. The Idaho organization rents out durable medical equipment, helping people with disabilities access their own homes.
stories.extended_summary: Melinda Nelson's knees are bone-on-bone from years of scrubbing floors as a hotel housekeeper. She needed a wheelchair ramp to get into her Weiser, Idaho, home, but insurance said no — the cost could hit $20,000. Then her doctor pointed her to Love INC, a local church group that rents out medical equipment including ramps. In the U.S., 6.8 million people rely on mobility devices, yet many face similar coverage gaps. Love INC's solution is simple and lifesaving.
stories.headline_split: Insurance Doesn’t Cover Wheelchair Ramps. In Idaho, a Church Group
stories.fake_headline: When Insurance Denied Her Surgery, a Small Town Crowdfunded the Full Cost

stories.title: KC’s Pruitt at forefront of flag football move by NJCAA
stories.summary: The NJCAA has elevated women's flag football to full championship status, thanks in part to Kilgore College's Dr. Courtney Pruitt. The sport grew from 15 colleges two years ago to 33, with an official national championship starting in 2027-28.
stories.extended_summary: Women's flag football is officially an NJCAA championship sport. Dr. Courtney Pruitt, athletic director at Kilgore College and the NJCAA's women's flag football sports chair, helped guide the sport through a rapid expansion. In just two years, the number of participating colleges more than doubled from 15 to 33. The Board of Regents approved the move, making flag football a fully sanctioned sport with an official national championship starting in the 2027-28 academic year. Pruitt called it a historic day for women's athletics.
stories.headline_split: KC’s Pruitt at forefront of flag football move
stories.fake_headline: Teen Golfer Breaks Record, Earns College Scholarship at Age 16

stories.title: Kids with autism are prone to drowning. Florida is trying to prevent that
stories.summary: Mackenzie Wesley, a 5-year-old with autism, loves water—but that love can be dangerous. Kids on the spectrum are 160 times more likely to drown. A Florida YMCA program gives kids like Mackenzie affordable swim lessons to prevent tragedy.
stories.extended_summary: Five-year-old Mackenzie Wesley beams in her Lilo & Stitch swimsuit as she dives into weekly lessons. She's one of many autistic kids drawn to water's sensory bliss—and one of the most at risk. In Florida, where water is everywhere, autistic children are 160 times more likely to drown than others. Swim Buddies, a YMCA program, offers low-cost lessons designed for kids with disabilities. It's a simple intervention that could save lives.

stories.title: Lewiston to launch first-in-the-nation program giving rent aid directly to tenants
stories.summary: Lewiston Housing launches the nation's first HUD-approved program giving rent aid directly to tenants via debit cards. The five-year pilot aims to reduce landlord discrimination and administrative overhead, letting 60 tenants pay rent without stigma.
stories.extended_summary: Rent assistance usually goes straight to landlords—but not anymore in Lewiston. A new HUD pilot gives 60 tenants a year restricted debit cards for rent or housing-related expenses. The goal: bypass landlord discrimination against voucher holders and cut bureaucracy. Deputy Director Travis Heynen says the program creates 'transparent assistance' so landlords may not even know tenants are getting help. If it works, it could reshape how housing aid is delivered nationwide.
stories.headline_split: Lewiston to launch first-in-the-nation program giving rent aid directly to

stories.title: Love Wins: Dueling Disco Raises More Than $104,000 for Local Children and Families (7-2-2026)
stories.summary: The Hike Kidz Foundation's Dueling Disco raised over $104,000 for local children and families, beating last year's total. The event featured dancing, costume contests, two donated vehicles, and a message that 'Love Wins.'
stories.extended_summary: Sequins and dance battles lit up Shenandoah Valley Golf Club, but the real win was the bottom line: $104,857 raised for kids and families in need. The second annual Dueling Disco topped last year's haul, funding clothing, essentials, and two cars donated through Cars Changing Lives. President Amber Veithenthal reminded the crowd that behind every disco ball is a real purpose—supporting local communities one dollar at a time.
stories.headline_split: Love Wins: Dueling Disco Raises More Than

stories.title: Meet the Founder Showing Black Entrepreneurs How to Build a Profitable Business in Just One Day
stories.summary: Dante Lee's Build Black Wealth program trains aspiring Black entrepreneurs to earn revenue in just one day. Participants learn to teach financial literacy in underserved communities, turning basic money skills into a weekly income stream.
stories.extended_summary: Dante Lee spent 27 years building businesses; now he's helping others do it in 30 minutes—literally. His Build Black Wealth initiative teaches participants a simple model: host financial literacy workshops on budgeting, credit, and saving. No startup capital or experience needed. Entrepreneurs get paid weekly plus bonuses, all while helping families in underserved communities build economic power. It's a fast track to both profit and purpose.

stories.title: Meet the Tree-Counting Juggernaut of New York City
stories.summary: Caleb Roenigk single-handedly surveyed 7,280 trees in New York City parks last year—more than 11% of all trees assessed. The volunteer tree-counter spent about 30 seconds per tree, making him the most prolific assessor in the city's deciennial census.
stories.extended_summary: In Bensonhurst Park, Caleb Roenigk approaches a London plane tree like he's sizing it for a suit. Tape measure out, phone ready—30 seconds later, he's onto the next. Between last summer and May, he repeated this ritual 7,280 times, far outrunning the second-place volunteer who clocked 4,689. Roenigk is responsible for over 11% of the 70,000 trees surveyed in NYC's parks. His dedication is giving the city an unmatched picture of its urban forest.
stories.headline_split: Meet the Tree-Counting
fake_endings: Expert Who Plants Forests on Rooftops

stories.title: Mesa County rescuers coordinate largest-ever evacuation of 123 campers as Snyder fire exploded near the Colorado River
stories.summary: As the Snyder fire exploded near the Colorado River, Mesa County rescuers evacuated 123 campers in the largest mission of its kind. Rail-riding trucks and deputies in motorboats coordinated overnight to pull rafters and dogs to safety while the fire grew 5,000 acres an hour.
stories.extended_summary: A small plume on the horizon turned into a nightmare for campers on the Ruby-Horsethief Canyon stretch of the Colorado River. By nightfall, ash was falling like rain and the Snyder fire was swelling by 5,000 acres an hour. Mesa County Search and Rescue launched its largest-ever evacuation, using Union Pacific trucks to pull 123 rafters (and several dogs) from the canyon. Caleb Weinberg and his crew—who had leaned into gusting winds and smoke all day—were among those who barely made it out. No one was hurt, thanks to a rescue that was equal parts speed and grace.
stories.headline_split: Mesa County rescuers coordinate largest-ever evacuation of 123

stories.title: Minnesota Youth Choir Shares a Heartfelt Gift this July Fourth
stories.summary: A 91-voice Minnesota Youth Honor Choir recorded 'We Are Your Children' for July Fourth. The song, available free to educators, aims to inspire 250 youth choirs nationwide and all proceeds support youth music programs.
stories.extended_summary: This Fourth of July, a musical gift arrives from 91 young voices. 'We Are Your Children' debuts on YouTube, written by Larry Sagan and arranged by Jason Hansen. The Minnesota Youth Honor Choir hopes the song will be sung by 250 community, school, and faith choirs during America's 250th anniversary year. Directors can download free arrangements and rehearsal tracks. Every stream and download supports nonprofits that fuel youth music and leadership. It's a present that keeps on giving.
stories.headline_split: Minnesota Youth Choir Shares a Heartfelt Gift this

stories.title: Native Group Rides Through Broadus On Journey Of Self Growth
stories.summary: Native youth from the Cheyenne River Sioux Tribe rode 372 miles on horseback from South Dakota to Montana for self-growth. The 17-day journey, part of the Wakinyan Wakpa Youth Horsemanship Program, builds confidence, teamwork, and connection to horses and heritage.
stories.extended_summary: Horseback riders crossed Highway 212 in Broadus, Montana—halfway through a 372-mile journey from Eagle Butte to Crow Agency. For 17 days, Native youth in the Wakinyan Wakpa program ride together, learning horsemanship and life skills. Kermit Jumping Eagle, the program's leader, says the long days in the saddle give young people time to connect with their horse, each other, and themselves. Some riders, like Josiah, had only been on a horse twice before. But by the end, they're making the charge at the Greasy Grass River carrying a deeper sense of purpose.
stories.headline_split: Native Group Rides Through Broadus On Journey Of

stories.title: New program helps youth in DCF care open their first bank accounts
stories.summary: Teens in Connecticut's DCF care opened their first bank accounts through a new Liberty Bank program. With no parent cosigner required, the accounts give young people financial independence and include fraud protection tips from college-age mentors.
stories.extended_summary: Inside Liberty Bank in Middletown, a group of teenagers in state care did something many of their peers take for granted: open a bank account. The pilot program removes the barrier of needing a parent or guardian's signature, giving 14- to 17-year-olds in DCF custody financial autonomy. They learned about fraud protection, ATM limits, and budgeting from college interns. CEO David Glidden shared his own unlikely path—from prison guard to bank CEO—and told the teens to try on different careers until they find what clicks. For Ari Ragston, 16, it was the first step toward a future he could control.
stories.headline_split: New program helps youth in DCF care open their first

stories.title: Not your traditional fireworks | Lopez volunteers continue to produce world-renowned show
stories.summary: The Lopez Island Community Fireworks show, run by volunteers since 1951, is one of the largest on the West Coast and the only Japanese-style display in the U.S. Director Nick Gislason carries on a tradition of artful pyrotechnics that brings the small island together.
stories.extended_summary: On a tiny island in the San Juan Islands, a fireworks show has become a world-class tradition. The Lopez Island Community Fireworks, entirely volunteer-run since 1951, is one of the five biggest on the West Coast—and the only Japanese-style display in America. Nick Gislason, an organic farmer and winemaker, took over from Dwight Walters, who learned from a Japanese master who rebuilt Nagaoka's pyrotechnic art after WWII. Every burst is crafted with intention, not military-style noise. For generations, islanders have gathered to watch a sky full of art, all built by neighbors. That's the real firework.
stories.headline_split: Not your traditional fireworks | Lopez volunteers continue to produce
